---
title: "Week 2 — Facilitator Notes"
subtitle: "Literate Programming for Reproducible Research"
---

## Learning Objectives

Residents should connect Week 1's philosophical analysis (reproducibility crisis, Retraction Watch) to concrete technical practice. They should leave able to create a Quarto document that is transparent, version-controlled, and reproducible. This session bridges "why" and "how."

---

## Session Format

| Segment | Duration | Activity |
|---------|----------|----------|
| Connection | 10 min | Recap Week 1: which failure modes would literate programming have prevented? |
| Lecture | 15 min | The reproducibility stack; chunk options; YAML as documentation |
| Live Demo | 20 min | Build the exercise document together, render, inspect HTML |
| Independent Exercise | 30 min | Residents complete Part 1–3 alone; facilitator circulates |
| Reflection & Share | 15 min | Part 4: residents share one Retraction Watch connection |
| Check-in | 10 min | Verify GitHub pushes; preview Week 3 (data structures) |

---

## Key Teaching Points

### Connect Week 1 to Week 2

Begin with the question: "Of the papers you analyzed last week, which failures would have been caught if the authors had published their code in a Quarto document?"

Expected answers:
- **Statistical error:** visible code; reviewers can re-run
- **Unavailable data:** the `.qmd` file shows exactly what was done, even if raw data is restricted
- **P-hacking:** version control shows all analyses attempted, not just reported ones

This connection is the **motivational bridge** for the entire tier. If residents don't feel this link, they will see Quarto as a chore, not a protection.

### The "Echo: True" Default

Many residents will want to hide code (`echo: false`) because it looks "cleaner." Push back explicitly:

> "In this course, `echo: true` is the default. If you hide your code, you are doing exactly what the retracted papers did — asking readers to trust your prose description. We don't trust. We verify."

Exceptions are allowed for:
- Final capstone submission (after peer review confirms correctness)
- Patient-facing output (where code would confuse)
- Facilitator-approved cases

### Inline Expressions Prevent Stale Text

Demonstrate the failure mode:

1. Write: "The mean MPG is 20.1." (hard-coded)
2. Change the data filter
3. Re-render
4. The text still says 20.1, but the code output shows 18.3

Then show the fix: `` `r round(mean(mtcars$mpg), 1)` `` — text updates automatically.

This is a **small teaching moment with large consequences.** Residents who learn this now will not have inconsistent manuscripts later.

### GitHub Push Verification

Many residents will have forgotten Git commands from Intro Week 2. Have a cheat sheet ready:

```bash
git status          # What's changed?
git add filename    # Stage
git commit -m "message"  # Commit
git push            # Push
```

Common failures:
- Not in a Git repository (forgot `git init`)
- No remote configured (forgot to create GitHub repo)
- SSH key not in agent (`ssh-add` needed)

---

## Live Demo Script

**Build the document step by step:**

1. Create new Quarto doc in RStudio
2. Show YAML header — explain each field
3. Add a prose section — "This is documentation"
4. Add a code chunk — `data(mtcars); head(mtcars)`
5. Render — show HTML output
6. Add `message: false` — re-render, show cleaner output
7. Add inline expression — `` `r mean(mtcars$mpg)` ``
8. Re-render — show updated text

**Key pause points:**
- After YAML: "This header is the first thing a replicator sees."
- After first render: "Anyone can download this file and get the same output."
- After inline expression: "The text updates automatically. You cannot forget."

---

## Exercise Guidance

**Part 1:** Residents often struggle with YAML indentation (spaces vs tabs). Walk the room early.

**Part 2:** The `mtcars` dataset is built into R — no package installation needed. This isolates the exercise to Quarto mechanics, not data wrangling.

**Part 3:** Git push is the most common failure point. Have residents verify their push by showing the file on GitHub in a browser.

**Part 4:** Reflection quality varies. Look for:
- Specific connection to a paper they analyzed (not generic)
- Recognition that transparency is protection
- Commitment to using this workflow in their own work

---

## Troubleshooting During Session

| Problem | Quick Fix |
|---------|-----------|
| YAML render error | Check indentation (spaces, not tabs); check colon spacing |
| "Object not found" | Code chunk not executed before inline expression; re-render |
| Git "not a repository" | `git init` in `~/mds-work`, then `git remote add origin ...` |
| Git push fails | Check `git remote -v`; verify SSH key with `ssh -T git@github.com` |
| HTML looks wrong | Check that `.qmd` file was saved before rendering |
| Inline expression shows raw text | Missing backtick-r syntax; check for spaces inside backticks |

---

## Assessment

Verify each resident can:

- [ ] Explain one Retraction Watch failure that literate programming prevents
- [ ] Create a `.qmd` with YAML, prose, code, and inline expressions
- [ ] Render to HTML without errors
- [ ] Push `.qmd` and `.html` to GitHub
- [ ] Identify why `echo: true` is the default in this course

If not, schedule 1-on-1 before Week 3.

---

## Preparation Checklist

- [ ] Tested RStudio Quarto template on classroom machines
- [ ] Prepared Git cheat sheet handout
- [ ] Selected 2–3 Week 1 Retraction Watch papers to reference
- [ ] Verified classroom WiFi allows GitHub SSH access
- [ ] Identified residents who may need Git refresher

---

## Connection to Week 3

Preview: Week 3 introduces data structures and tidy data. You will take the Quarto skills from this week and apply them to real (messy) clinical data. The document structure is the same; the data is more complex.

End session with: "Week 1 told you why reproducibility matters. Week 2 gave you the tool. Week 3 starts applying it to anesthesia data."
